This shows that he was well acquainted with Ayurveda also.
His profound scholarship in astronomy and mathematics is
attested to by the calculations and quotations he has given in
Chapter II. Therein he quotes profusely the Brahmasiddhdnta^
Paiicasiddhdniika, Pauliia-siddhSnta, Aryabhaf.a, Bhapla Balabhadra,
Brahmagnpta and Garga about the elements of calendar {yugatic.),
whose smallest unit is J Truti. It is surmised that he has written an
independent Karam grantha. Under 11.12 he explains that the earth
hangs in space like an iro^t ball held steady between two magnets.
He tells us, in this connection, about the divergence of opinion
among the Acdryas regarding the 'lord of the day and Hora.3
His extraordinary proficiency in architecture is clearly proved
by his quotations and intimate knowledge of the various details of
the construction of houses and temples as well as iconography. He
supplements many statements of the Acarya with lucid explanations
and apt quotations.
